codeSnapshot.label: roc_auc.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.metrics import (
    roc_curve, roc_auc_score, RocCurveDisplay,
    average_precision_score, precision_recall_curve
)
from sklearn.datasets import make_classification
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

# Binary ROC-AUC
X, y = make_classification(n_samples=1000, n_features=20, weights=[0.7, 0.3],
                            random_state=42)
clf = LogisticRegression()
clf.fit(X[:800], y[:800])
scores = clf.predict_proba(X[800:])[:, 1]  # positive class probabilities
y_test = y[800:]

# ROC curve
fpr, tpr, thresholds = roc_curve(y_test, scores)
# fpr[i] = FPR at threshold thresholds[i]
# tpr[i] = TPR (Recall) at threshold thresholds[i]

auc = roc_auc_score(y_test, scores)
print(f'AUC: {auc:.4f}')
# AUC = P(score(positive) > score(negative)) — probabilistic interpretation

# Probabilistic interpretation of AUC
n_pos = (y_test == 1).sum()
n_neg = (y_test == 0).sum()
pos_scores = scores[y_test == 1]
neg_scores = scores[y_test == 0]
auc_empirical = np.mean([sp > sn for sp in pos_scores for sn in neg_scores])
print(f'AUC (empirical): {auc_empirical:.4f}')  # matches roc_auc_score

# Multi-class AUC (OvR — one-vs-rest)
from sklearn.datasets import make_classification
from sklearn.multiclass import OneVsRestClassifier
X_mc, y_mc = make_classification(n_samples=1000, n_classes=3, n_features=20,
                                  n_informative=10, random_state=42)
clf_mc = OneVsRestClassifier(LogisticRegression())
clf_mc.fit(X_mc[:800], y_mc[:800])
scores_mc = clf_mc.predict_proba(X_mc[800:])  # (200, 3)
auc_macro = roc_auc_score(y_mc[800:], scores_mc, multi_class='ovr', average='macro')
print(f'Multi-class AUC (macro): {auc_macro:.4f}')

# PR-AUC for imbalanced data
ap = average_precision_score(y_test, scores)  # average precision = PR-AUC
print(f'PR-AUC (Average Precision): {ap:.4f}')

eli3: The ROC curve (Receiver Operating Characteristic) shows how well a classifier separates positives from negatives across all possible decision thresholds. Each point on the curve is a different threshold — how strict you are about calling something positive.

AUC (Area Under the Curve) summarises this into one number: the probability that a randomly chosen positive example gets a higher score than a randomly chosen negative example. AUC=0.5 means the model is random; AUC=1.0 means perfect separation.

ROC-AUC is threshold-independent — it evaluates the model's discriminative ability regardless of where you set the cutoff. This makes it ideal for comparing classifiers, while the confusion matrix tells you how well a specific threshold works.

whenWhy.title: Comparing classifiers regardless of threshold
whenWhy.body: Different applications require different operating points (high recall vs high precision), making threshold-dependent metrics (accuracy, F1 at a fixed threshold) hard to compare across classifiers. ROC-AUC evaluates performance across all thresholds simultaneously, giving a single number that captures the classifier's overall discriminative ability. Standard in medical research (diagnosis), information retrieval (ranking), and credit scoring. AUC is also invariant to class priors — changing the class ratio doesn't change AUC.

whenWhy.title: Choosing between ROC-AUC and PR-AUC
whenWhy.body: For balanced or mildly imbalanced datasets: ROC-AUC is the standard. For severely imbalanced datasets (positive rate <5%): PR-AUC (average precision) is more informative. Reason: ROC-AUC can be high even when the model produces many false positives, because the FPR denominator (TN+FP) is dominated by the large number of true negatives. PR-AUC directly measures performance on positives: precision (of predicted positives) vs recall (of actual positives). Davis & Goadrich (2006): there is a one-to-one correspondence between points on the ROC and PR curves, but PR curve reveals more information in the high-recall, low-precision regime.

whenWhy.title: AUC for ranking and scoring systems
whenWhy.body: AUC = P(score(pos) > score(neg)) — the Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney statistic. This means AUC directly measures ranking quality: how often the classifier ranks a positive above a negative. For recommender systems, fraud detection, and credit scoring, what matters is correct ranking (relative order of scores), not the absolute probability calibration. A model with AUC=0.95 correctly ranks 95% of (positive, negative) pairs — useful even if probability estimates are uncalibrated.

deepDiveIntro: The ROC curve traces the trade-off between true positive rate and false positive rate as the decision threshold is swept from permissive to strict. Each point on the curve corresponds to one threshold. AUC equals the probability that a randomly chosen positive example receives a higher model score than a randomly chosen negative example — a clean probabilistic statement about ranking quality that does not depend on any particular threshold. [1]

AUC has two important invariance properties. First, it does not change under any monotone transformation of the scores, so only the ranking of scores matters. Second, it does not change when the ratio of positives to negatives in the test set changes, unlike accuracy or F1 at a fixed threshold. A classifier scoring 0.5 AUC is no better than random; a classifier with AUC below 0.5 is reliably wrong and can be fixed by flipping its predictions. [1]

deepDive.heading: Computing the ROC Curve and AUC
deepDive.body: Algorithm: (1) Sort test examples by score descending. (2) Starting at (FPR=0,TPR=0), iterate through sorted examples: if positive, move up (TPR += 1/n₊); if negative, move right (FPR += 1/n₋). (3) AUC = area under the piecewise-linear curve. Trapezoidal rule: AUC = Σᵢ (FPRᵢ - FPRᵢ₋₁)·(TPRᵢ + TPRᵢ₋₁)/2. The equivalent formula using ranks: AUC = (U/(n₊·n₋)) where U = Σᵢ:yᵢ=1 rank(xᵢ) - n₊(n₊+1)/2 (Wilcoxon rank-sum statistic). [1]
deepDive.formula: AUC = P(score(x₊) > score(x₋)) where x₊ ~ Positive, x₋ ~ Negative

deepDive.heading: Partial AUC and Operating Constraints
deepDive.body: Full AUC integrates over all FPR values [0,1], but for many applications only a specific FPR range matters (e.g., FPR ≤ 0.01 for rare event detection). Partial AUC: pAUC = ∫₀^{fpr_max} TPR(FPR) d(FPR). sklearn: roc_auc_score(max_fpr=0.1) computes pAUC over [0, 0.1]. Normalise by dividing by fpr_max to get a [0,1] metric. Medical diagnostics: often report sensitivity at fixed specificity (e.g., 'sensitivity at 95% specificity') — a single point on the ROC curve — rather than the full AUC. [1]

deepDive.heading: Multi-Class AUC
deepDive.body: Multi-class AUC requires specifying an averaging strategy: (1) OvR (One-vs-Rest): compute AUC for each class against all others, then average. Macro-OvR: equal weight per class; Weighted-OvR: weight by class prevalence. (2) OvO (One-vs-One, Hand & Till, 2001): compute AUC for each pair of classes, average over all pairs. OvO is more informative for imbalanced multi-class problems (not dominated by majority class pairs). sklearn: roc_auc_score(..., multi_class='ovr', average='macro') or multi_class='ovo'. [1]

youtubeLabel: ROC Curves and AUC — Explained Simply
takeaways: AUC = P(score(pos) > score(neg)) — probability of correct ranking of a pos/neg pair. [1]
AUC is invariant to monotone score transformations and class imbalance changes. [1]
ROC curve: TPR vs FPR across all thresholds; AUC = area under this curve. [1]
Prefer PR-AUC over ROC-AUC when positive class is rare (<5%) — ROC can be misleadingly high. [2]
AUC=0.5 is random; AUC=1.0 is perfect; AUC<0.5 means flip predictions. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why does a high AUC not guarantee a useful classifier?
edgeCases.a: AUC measures ranking ability — it does not guarantee that the classifier produces well-calibrated probabilities. A classifier that ranks all positives above all negatives has AUC=1.0, but if it outputs scores of 0.51 for positives and 0.49 for negatives, the threshold-dependent metrics (precision, recall at threshold=0.5) are still poor. Additionally, AUC averages over all thresholds, including extreme ones that are never used in practice. For high-FPR operating points (e.g., FPR=0.9), a classifier can boost AUC by improving TPR where it doesn't matter. If your application only operates at a specific FPR, use partial AUC or sensitivity/specificity at that operating point.

edgeCases.q: How does AUC relate to the Gini coefficient used in credit scoring?
edgeCases.a: The Gini coefficient in credit scoring is defined as Gini = 2·AUC - 1. It rescales AUC from [0.5, 1] to [0, 1], where 0 is random and 1 is perfect. This is equivalent to the Somers' D statistic (concordance minus discordance divided by total pairs). In credit scoring, a Gini > 0.4 (AUC > 0.7) is considered acceptable; Gini > 0.6 (AUC > 0.8) is good. The Gini coefficient in this context is not the same as the Gini impurity used in decision trees — it's a ranking quality measure derived directly from the ROC curve.

sources.label: Hanley & McNeil (1982) — The Meaning and Use of the Area under a Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve, Radiology
Davis & Goadrich (2006) — The Relationship Between Precision-Recall and ROC Curves, ICML 2006
